coredata.dc:title: Aggregation induced emission carbon dots with chiral and circularly polarized luminescent properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Liming
Wang, Rui
Peng, Yanlei
Huang, Shulin
Han, Yalin
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel luminescent carbon material, are expected to be excellent candidates for constructing circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) materials. However, the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon of CDs hinders the generation of CPL in the aggregated state. Herein, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs with chiral and CPL properties were fabricated by using dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and L/D-glutamic acid as precursors. Interestingly, the obtained CDs do not exhibit the desired chiral and CPL properties when dispersed in solution. In this case, due to molecular motion on the surface, excessive non-radiative energy is consumed, which subsequently reduces the surface radiation luminescence and shows the blue luminescence of carbon nuclei. Meanwhile, the relatively disordered molecular conformation prevents them from displaying chiral and CPL characteristics. Conversely, in the aggregated state, the intramolecular rotation (IR) of the disulfide bond (S-S) axis is restricted, thereby inducing a strong solid-state fluorescence and red-shifted emission. Concomitantly, alterations in molecular conformation, interactions between molecules, and the chiral functional groups intrinsic to the synthesized CDs may result in the manifestation of chiral and CPL properties. Furthermore, the synthesized AIE CDs were successfully applied to color-changing gels and fingerprint detection, and they can also impart chirality and CPL to PVA membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission
Chirality
Circularly polarized luminescence
Discoloration hydrogel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Applications of multi-color AIE CDs materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation process of the chiral AIE CDs. (b-c) TEM images of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0. (d-e) XRD and FT-IR spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0. (f) XPS spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0. (g-i) XPS high-resolution C 1 s, S 2p and O 1 s spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0.

(a-b) UV–vis absorption, optimal PL excitation and emission spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 in the dispersed state. (c-d) PL emission spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 in the dispersed state at different excitation wavelengths. (e-f) Excitation–emission matrix for L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 in the dispersed state. (g-h) UV–vis absorption, optimal PL excitation and emission spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 solid powder. (i-j) PL emission spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 solid powder under different excitation wavelengths. (k-l) Excitation–emission matrix for L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 solid powder.

(a) Images of L-CD2.0 dissolved in water/DMSO mixture solution under visible and UV light, respectively, with varying volume ratios of water (0 %-90 %). (b) Normalized emission spectra of L-CD2.0 under different water volume ratios. (c) Photographs of L-CD2.0 solutions with water/DMSO ratios of 40 %, 50 % and 70 % under bright field and UV light conditions in an upright fluorescence microscope. (d) Images of L-CD2.0 dissolved in ethanol with different concentrations (0.5, 2.5, 5, 20, 40 mg⋅mL⁻¹) under UV light. (e) Normalized emission spectra of L-CD2.0 with different concentrations (0.5, 2.5, 5, 20, 40 mg⋅mL⁻¹). (f) Spectral overlap of the absorption spectra of the concentrated solution (40 mg⋅mL⁻¹) with the emission spectra of the diluted solution (0.5 mg⋅mL⁻¹). (g) Schematic of AIE effect of CDs.

(a-b) CD and UV–vis spectra of L-CD0.2–2.0 and D-CD1.5–2.0. (c) Composite diagram of CD and UV–vis spectra of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0. (d) CD and UV–vis spectra of L-CD2.0 dispersed in solvents consisting of water and ethanol in different ratios. (e) Composite diagram of CD and UV–vis spectra of L-CD2.0@PVA and D-CD2.0@PVA membranes. (f) Composite diagram of CPL and corresponding PL emission spectra of L-CD2.0@PVA and D-CD2.0@PVA membranes. (g) Photographs of L-CD2.0 dispersed in water/ethanol solvent mixtures with water contents of 20 %, 40 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 90 % respectively, taken under bright-field and ultraviolet light conditions using an upright fluorescence microscope.

(a) Time-dependent photographs of L-CD2.0 color-changing hydrogel immersed in water and subsequently placed in ethanol. (b) Aggregation process images of L-CD2.0 in color-changing gel under fluorescence microscope. (c) The color-changing process of color changing ink. (d) L-CD2.0@TPU film logo attached to clothes under sunlight and UV light. (e) The potential fingerprint images with L-CD2.0 powder.

head.ce:title: Aggregation induced emission carbon dots with chiral and circularly polarized luminescent properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: New Dongyue Group Co., Ltd., Tai'an 271500, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: New Dongyue Group Co., Ltd., Tai'an 271500, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel luminescent carbon material, are expected to be excellent candidates for constructing circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) materials. However, the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon of CDs hinders the generation of CPL in the aggregated state. Herein, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs with chiral and CPL properties were fabricated by using dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and L/D-glutamic acid as precursors. Interestingly, the obtained CDs do not exhibit the desired chiral and CPL properties when dispersed in solution. In this case, due to molecular motion on the surface, excessive non-radiative energy is consumed, which subsequently reduces the surface radiation luminescence and shows the blue luminescence of carbon nuclei. Meanwhile, the relatively disordered molecular conformation prevents them from displaying chiral and CPL characteristics. Conversely, in the aggregated state, the intramolecular rotation (IR) of the disulfide bond (S-S) axis is restricted, thereby inducing a strong solid-state fluorescence and red-shifted emission. Concomitantly, alterations in molecular conformation, interactions between molecules, and the chiral functional groups intrinsic to the synthesized CDs may result in the manifestation of chiral and CPL properties. Furthermore, the synthesized AIE CDs were successfully applied to color-changing gels and fingerprint detection, and they can also impart chirality and CPL to PVA membranes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation and multi-application of AIE CDs with chiral and CPL properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, chiral materials and their related CPL have attracted considerable attention and made noteworthy progress in a number of fields, including chiral recognition, three-dimensional display, optoelectronic devices, information storage, and CPL lasers[1–4]. Carbon dots (CDs) are an excellent candidate as chiral materials due to their unique advantages, including tunable photoluminescence (PL), high stability, high quantum yield (QY), straightforward preparation, and favorable biocompatibility[5–7]. Nevertheless, most CDs are subject to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) due to the formation of strong π–π stacking interactions, which prevents chiral CDs from producing CPL in the aggregated state[8–10]. Researchers have found that dispersing CDs in solid matrices such as polymers and silica can suppress their ACQ. However, the materials prepared by this method usually have large particles and only show weak photoluminescence[11,12]. In 2001, Tang et al. reported the synthesis of luminescent compounds with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties[13]. The AIE effect is regarded as an effective strategy for resolving the dilemma of ACQ. Subsequently, many researchers applied the AIE theory to the synthesis of CDs and successfully prepared solid-state fluorescent (SSF) CDs[14,15]. The solid-state luminescence properties of AIE CDs are well suited for application in the circularly polarized luminescence of chiral materials. In 2013, Cheng et al. successfully synthesized chiral AIE CDs by introducing tyrosine side groups into tetrastyrene (TPE) fragments on the main chain, and reported the structure and optical properties of these CDs[16]. However, chiral AIE CDs with CPL performance have only received attention in recent years and the relevant research remains limited[17]. More challengingly, due to the spherical microscopic morphology of CDs and the random distribution of surface groups, it is difficult to achieve excited-state chiral emission. Therefore, chiral CDs usually only exhibit ground-state circular dichroism (CD) absorption without excited-state CPL. In addition, the currently reported CDs with CPL performances are mainly focused in the short-wave region and show low luminescence asymmetry factor (glum) values (10−5-10−3)[18,19].

Accordingly, this study employed dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) as a carbon source and constructed chiral AIE CDs with CPL properties by incorporating L/D-Glu into the system. In the dispersed state, the non-radiative energy consumption due to molecular motion on the surface results in a reduction in surface radiative luminescence, which is manifested as blue luminescence of carbon nuclei. Conversely, in the aggregated state, the intramolecular rotation (IR) of the S-S bonds axis in the axially symmetric heterocyclic compound is restrained and the non-radiative transitions in CDs are suppressed, thus leading to a strong red-shifted fluorescence. Meanwhile, the molecular conformation changes from a relatively disordered state to a specific self-assembled morphology. This causes CDs with initially ambiguous chirality to exhibit chiral characteristics or intensify the existing chiral signals, and even generate CPL. Moreover, the chirality and CPL properties have been successfully incorporated into PVA films. Further research has been conducted on the potential applications of chiral AIE CDs in information encryption and fingerprint recognition.

In this work, AIE CDs with chirality were prepared via a one-pot solvothermal reaction of DTSA and L/D-Glu. Interestingly, the prepared CDs present blue fluorescence in the dispersed state, while pink fluorescence in the aggregated state. Moreover, the solid-state CDs possess CD and CPL signals ( Fig. 1a). It should be noted that DTSA plays a crucial role in the obtained CDs displaying AIE and solid-state fluorescence (SSF) characteristics, mainly due to its unique structure of S-S bonds that form symmetric molecules[20,21]. When L/D-Glu was used alone as a precursor under the same reaction conditions, the resulting CDs (named CDs-L and CDs-D) did not exhibit AIE and SSF characteristics (Fig. S1), but they do have chirality (Fig. S2). When L/D-Glu is not added, no CD signal can be observed. As shown in Fig. S2, circular dichroism (CD) spectra were obtained for both the precursor (L/D-Glu) and the synthesized CDs. The characteristic CD signals of L-Glu and D-Glu were clearly distinguishable. For the CDs, they exhibited mirror-image CD signals in the wavelength range of 200–240 nm, which was consistent with the chiral characteristics of their respective precursors. This indicates that the chirality of the CDs is closely related to that of the Glu precursor. The main difference lies in the intensity of the CD signals. Additionally, after the carbonization process, the peaks of CD spectra shift from 202 to 210 nm, likely due to the formation of new chiral centers during the polymerization and carbonization reactions [22].

Furthermore, the amount of L/D-Glu was systematically investigated. 0.2 g of DTSA reacted with different masses of L-Glu (0.2, 0.6, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 g) under similar conditions to obtain AIE CDs, correspondingly named L-CD0.2, L-CD0.6, L-CD1.0, L-CD1.5, and L-CD2.0. L-Glu was replaced with D-Glu (1.5, 2.0 g), and the resulting AIE CDs were named D-CD1.5 and D-CD2.0.

In order to gain a deeper understanding of their structure, a comprehensive analysis was conducted using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM images show that the typical samples of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 are spheroid-like particles with an average diameter of 7 nm. High-resolution images of these particles clearly demonstrate the presence of lattice stripes. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the lattice plane of graphite carbon (100) (Fig. 1b-c)[22,23]. XRD spectra display sharp diffraction peaks, with notable carbon peaks observed at 2θ= 26° (Fig. 1d and Fig. S3), suggesting the favorable crystallinity of the prepared solid-state CDs and the presence of graphite carbon nuclei. Similar results are also observed in CDs synthesized in previous studies[24,25]. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1e, Fig. S4) illustrate the presence of functional groups or chemical bands of L-CDs and D-CDs, including N-H/O-H (3320–3700 cm−1), S-H (2660 cm−1), CN (1680 cm−1), CC (1560 cm−1), C-O (1410 cm−1), C-N (1270 cm−1), C-H (730 cm−1), C-S (696 cm−1), and S-S (556 cm−1)[26,27]. Furthermore, the full survey XPS spectra reveal four distinct elements of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S) (Fig. 1f) and the proportion of these four elements is presented in Fig. S5. Subsequently, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 revealed the presence of C-C/CC (284.6 eV), C-O/C-N (286.2 eV), and CO/CN (288.5 eV) functional groups (Fig. 1g)[28]. From the S 2p high-resolution XPS spectra, S-C (163.3 eV) and S-S (164.5 eV) can be observed (Fig. 1h)[29]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s exhibit two distinct peaks, which are attributed to CO (531.6 eV) and C-O (533.1 eV), respectively (Fig. 1i)[30]. Based on the above results, heteroatoms N and S were successfully doped into CDs. Especially for the S-S bond, its intramolecular rotation is impeded, thereby enabling the dissipation of energy through surface radiation and the fulfillment of AIE. Meanwhile, CDs inherit part of the precursor structure, which is crucial for retaining the chiral structure to generate chiral CDs.

Subsequently, the optical properties of L/D-CDs were thoroughly investigated. The UV–visible absorption spectra of L/D-CDs in the dispersed state ( Fig. 2a-b, Fig. S6 and Fig. S7) are largely similar, exhibiting distinct absorption peaks with comparable peak widths and intensities at approximately 220 nm, 250 nm, and 310 nm, which are attributed to the π–π* transition and the large conjugate structure of the CDs. The optimal excitation wavelength is around 360 nm, and the corresponding optimal emission wavelength is about 400 nm, showing blue photoluminescence and evident excitation dependence features (Fig. 2c-f, Fig. S8 and Fig. S9), which mainly derive from the luminescence of the carbon core of ordinary CDs[31]. Comparatively, L/D-CDs in the aggregated state exhibit a different broad absorption (440–590 nm), resulting from the n–π* transitions of the surface states caused by the CN/CO, C-O, C-S, and S-S structures (Fig. 2g-h, Fig. S10 and Fig. S11)[32,33]. In particular, this broad absorption band continuously intensifies with an increase in the amount of glutamic acid (Fig. S10 and Fig. S11). Additionally, L/D-CDs powders present a notable expansion in the excitation range. The optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength redshift to 550 nm and 585 nm, respectively, resulting in a solid-state pink luminescence. Fig. S12 more intuitively presents the data comparison of the optimal emission spectra of L/D-CDs powder. Notably, L/D-CDs powders exhibit unobvious excitation dependence (Fig. 2i-l, Fig. S13) and two remarkable emission centers at the excitation wavelength 330–450 nm. According to the current proposed luminescence mechanism, there are two emission centers, namely intrinsic state emission of carbon core and surface functional group emission. That is, in the dispersed state, L/D-CDs exhibit short-wave emission mainly from the intrinsic state emission of the carbon core. While in the aggregated state, long-wave emission is mainly dominated by surface defects and functional groups[34,35]. The process of short-wave emission in a dispersed state, which alters the luminescence characteristics after aggregation and shifts towards longer wavelengths, is referred to as “aggregation-induced conversion emission” [36,37]. The fluorescence quantum yields of L-CD2.0 and D-CD2.0 in their dispersed states are 7.32 % and 7.81 %, respectively, while in the aggregated state they are 17.15 % and 18.24 %, respectively.

In order to better investigate the aggregation induction process, we carried out a detailed study with L-CD2.0 as a reference. Fig. 3a shows that L-CD2.0 has good solubility in DMSO but is insoluble in water. When the water content is less than 50 %, the solution is in a transparent state and emits blue fluorescence under UV light. When the water content reaches 50 %, the solutions begin to turn turbid, emit pink fluorescence and sink to the bottom after standing still, indicating the formation of aggregates with the addition of the poor solvent. The transmittance of these solutions continues to decrease (Fig. S14). From Fig. 3b, it can be seen that when the water content is less than 50 % there is a single emission peak around 440 nm. As the water content increases to 50 %, a second emission peak appears at 585 nm, and gradually becomes stronger with the increase of the water content. In order to better observe the aggregation induction process, the samples with water contents of 40 %, 50 % and 70 % were observed under a fluorescence microscope (Fig. 3c). Obviously, no obvious particles were observed under a water content of 40 %. While aggregated CDs particles appear under a water content of 50 %, accompanied by fluorescence. There are more and larger clusters of CDs particles under a water content of 70 %, and the fluorescence is also more pronounced. Furthermore, the ethanol solution of L-CD2.0 was observed under a fluorescence microscope (Fig. S15, Video 1). It is worth noting that with the continuous evaporation of ethanol, more and more aggregated CDs particles with bright fluorescence appear, further proving the AIE effect. To further elucidate the AIE redshift mechanism, the effect of L-CD2.0 concentration was investigated (Fig. 3d-f). Remarkably, the fluorescence spectra of L-CD2.0 display an obvious peak at around 450 nm, which red-shifts from 450 nm to 470 nm with the increase of concentration from 0.2 mg⋅mL⁻¹ to 40 mg⋅mL⁻¹ , accompanied by a gradually increasing weak peak at 585 nm (Fig. 3e). Additionally, the absorption spectra exhibited a notable broadening and red-shift with increasing concentration of L-CD2.0 (Fig. S16a). Subsequently, the absorption spectrum was converted into a plot of (αhν)2 versus hν, thereby further determining the optical bandgap (Eg), where α, h, and ν represent the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, and optical frequency, respectively. The Eg value corresponds to the intercept on the x-axis (Fig. S16b). It can be observed that the Eg values decreased with increasing concentration, reaching 3.45 eV, 3.38 eV, 3.29 eV, 3.02 eV and 2.11 eV, respectively. This indicates that the concentration has an effect on the optical bandgap of the CDs, which may be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomenon[38]. Generally speaking, the significant overlap (usually greater than 30 %) between the emission spectrum of dilute solutions and the absorption spectrum of concentrated solutions is an important factor determining whether FRET occurs in fluorescence systems. The overlap rate between the absorption spectrum of the concentrated solution (40 mg⋅mL⁻¹) and the emission spectrum of the diluted solution (0.2 mg⋅mL⁻¹) was calculated up to 69.66 % (Fig. 3f), which is greater than the FRET-required overlap rate (30 %). Therefore, it can be inferred that the effect of concentration on the optical bandgap of CDs is attributed to the FRET phenomenon[39,40]. We suppose that when the CDs are in the dispersed state, their surface molecular motion consumes excessive non-radiative energy, reducing the surface radiative luminescence and exhibiting the blue luminescence of carbon nuclei. In the aggregated state, the prepared AIE CDs exhibit significant SSF properties. This phenomenon is not common because CDs usually consume transition energy due to π–π conjugation stacking, leading to fluorescence quenching[41]. However, the presence of S-S bonds greatly increases the hydrophobicity of CDs, leading to their aggregation behavior. This aggregation effect further limits the IR process of the disulfide bond axes in axisymmetric heterocyclic compounds, which complies with the AIE phenomenon[20]. At the same time, non-radiative transitions in CDs are suppressed due to the constrained motion or vibration of surface molecules, which greatly enhances the surface state fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 3g).
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136887.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1

The chirality of the CDs in aggregated state was characterized by circular dichroism (CD) ( Fig. 4a-c, Fig. S17). It is noteworthy that the CD signal of L-CDs only begins to appear when the amount of L-Glu is increased up to 1 g, and then keeps getting stronger (Fig. 4a), and a similar trend for D-CDs (Fig. 4b). This is because more Glu molecules are involved in the dehydration polymerization reaction as the amount increases. During this process, new chiral centers may be formed due to the complex chemical reactions such as cross-linking and cyclization[22]. Moreover, L/D-CD2.0 displays strong mirror-image CD signals at 200–240 nm, which is consistent with the corresponding absorption spectra (Fig. 4c). The mirror-image CD signals of L-CDs and D-CDs suggest that the basic chiral configuration from the Glu is maintained. We believe that the carbon-carbon and carbon-heteroatom bonds formed during the dehydration polymerization process of Glu play a crucial role in this retention. For example, the amino and carboxyl groups of Glu participate in the polymerization reaction, and the spatial arrangement of the chiral carbon in Glu is preserved in the polymerized structure of the CDs.

It is interesting to note that CDs in the dispersed state did not show CD signals. To further illustrate this, CD tests were performed on CDs dispersed in different proportions of ethanol and water (Fig. 4d). When the water content is less than 50 %, there is no CD signal, but there are three strong absorption peaks in the 200–350 nm. When the water content reaches 50 %, a weak CD signal appears. This may be caused by the few aggregated CDs (Fig. 3a). However, the absorption spectra remain the same as before due to the dominance of the dispersed CDs. When the water content reaches 60 %-90 %, the CD signal becomes significantly stronger due to the more aggregated CDs. Meanwhile, the absorption spectra change from being dominated by the dispersed state to being dominated by the aggregated state. We suppose that the molecular conformation of the dispersed CDs may be relatively disordered, however, after adding a poor solvent (water), CDs will self-assemble and aggregate. During the aggregation process, the interactions between CDs will lead to changes in molecular conformation. This conformational change may cause CDs that originally do not have obvious chirality to develop chiral characteristics or enhance existing chiral signals. Moreover, in the aggregated state, there are various intermolecular interactions between CDs, such as π–π stacking, hydrogen bonding, and van der Waals forces. These interactions can affect the electronic structure and energy level distribution of CDs, thus influencing their circularly polarized luminescence[42,43]. In addition, the synthesized CDs themselves have chiral functional groups, further enhancing the CD signal of CDs. In order to confirm this argument, the aggregation process of the material was monitored to clarify the changes in its molecular conformation. Fluorescence microscope images (Fig. 4 g) indicated that upon the gradual addition of poor solvents (water), the material showed continuous self-assembly and aggregation into different morphologies, from spheres (20 %-40 % water) to short rods (50 % water), more and more compact stacking (70 %-90 % water). This is ascribed to the functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. 1e) that are capable of forming hydrogen bonds[44,45]. The poor transparency of CDs in the aggregated state and the severe light scattering problem of the powder samples hinder the direct measurement of their CD and CPL signals. To avoid these problems, L/D-CD2.0 was dispersed in poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) to obtain the L-CD2.0@PVA and D-CD2.0@PVA membranes, and CD and CPL tests were conducted on these membranes. L-CD2.0@PVA and D-CD2.0@PVA have similar absorption spectra and mirrored CD signals (Fig. 4e). Among them, the CD signal between 240 nm and 290 nm may be caused by the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain in a chiral environment. The CD signal between 290 nm and 400 nm may be caused by the n–π* transition of the surface states[46]. As shown in Fig. 4 f, L-CD2.0@PVA and D-CD2.0@PVA exhibit excellent CPL symmetry signals near a wavelength of 590 nm, with a luminescence asymmetry factor of glum of 0.243. These results indicate that the preparation of chiral AIE CDs with circularly polarized luminescence is successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of multi-color AIE CDs materials
ce:section.ce:para: The unique optical properties of AIE CDs mean that they can be applied in many aspects. First, fluorescent color-changing hydrogels were prepared with L-CD2.0 and agar. Initially, the CDs were dispersed in the hydrogel in an aggregated state, presenting red fluorescence. As the gel was left in ethanol, ethanol continuously diffused into the interior of the hydrogel and replaced water. The aggregated CDs from the periphery to the center of the gel gradually changed to a dispersed state, and the gel color also changed from pink to blue. The discolored gel was then placed in water again, and water began to replace ethanol. The dispersed state CDs from the periphery of the gel gradually changed to an aggregated state, and the color also changed from blue to pink again ( Fig. 5a). This process can be clearly observed under a fluorescence microscope (Fig. 5b, Video 2). Furthermore, the prepared CDs can be used as fluorescent ink to draw various patterns (Fig. 5c). These unique reversible color change behaviors can be further used to encode information. Additionally, pink fluorescence L-CD2.0@TPU film was fabricated, and the fluorescent film can be made into patterns and affixed to clothes. It can serve as both a decorative element and a warning sign during nocturnal hours (Fig. 5d). Additionally, based on the good lipid solubility of CDs, we tested their ability to detect fingerprints, and successfully detected fingerprints on glass, rulers, plastic sheets and kitchen knives. As shown in Fig. 5e, the fingerprints display orange-red fluorescence, which makes the details and features of the fingerprint clearer, including core, bifurcation, independent ridge, spur, scar and sweat pores.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136887.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2

In summary, AIE CDs with chiral and CPL properties have been successfully prepared by solvothermal method using DTSA and L/D-Glu as precursors. Interestingly, in the dispersed state, due to excessive consumption of non-radiative energy by surface molecular motion, the luminescence of carbon cores dominates and shows blue fluorescence. Meanwhile, the relatively disordered molecular conformation may result in the absence of chirality and CPL properties. Conversely, in the aggregated state, the surfaces’ intramolecular rotation is constrained around S-S bonds, which significantly enhances the fluorescence of the surface state, resulting in a pink fluorescence. During the aggregation process, the interactions among CDs can lead to changes in molecular conformation, further triggering the chirality of CDs or enhancing the existing chiral signals, as well as generating circularly polarized luminescence, especially L/D-CD2.0@PVA membranes. Subsequently, the color-changing property was employed in the preparation of color-changing gels and inks, both of which have the potential for further application in anti-counterfeiting technologies. Additionally, L/D-CD2.0 can be applied in fingerprint detection, presenting the details of fingerprints more clearly. These results demonstrate that the prepared CDs have broad application prospects.

Wang Rui: Data curation. Fu Liming: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Huang Shulin: Data curation. Peng Yanlei: Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhao Xihui: Writing – review & editing. Han Yalin: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declared that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted. The contents of this manuscript have not been copyrighted or published previously. The contents of this manuscript are not now under consideration for publication elsewhere.
I am one author signing on behalf of all co-authors of this manuscript, and attesting to the above.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022ME159) and State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41 and No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136887.
